elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a new class of neural networks designed to be convex functions of their inputs, leveraging the principle that any convex function can be represented as the supremum of the affine functions it dominates.
elements.sent-2411-002: These neural networks, inherently convex with respect to their inputs, are particularly well-suited for approximating the prices of options with convex payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-003: We detail the architecture of this, and establish theoretical convergence bounds that validate its approximation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also introduce a scrambling phase to improve the training of these networks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, we demonstrate numerically the effectiveness of these networks in estimating prices for three types of options with convex payoffs: Basket, Bermudan, and Swing options.
elements.sent-2411-006: Keywords - Input Convex neural network, Options pricing, Convex functions approximation, Basket options, Optimal stopping, Stochastic optimal control.
elements.sent-2411-007: Neural networks have become powerful tools for function and representation approximation.
elements.sent-2411-008: In many fields, such as finance, the target function to approximate is often convex, and preserving this convexity is essential, as failing to do so can lead to financial losses because of arbitrage opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this paper, we introduce a novel class of convex networks: neural networks specifically structured to yield convex functions of their inputs.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is worth noticing that, to this end, some approaches have already been developed.
elements.sent-2411-011: Among others, one may find Input Convex Neural Networks (ICNN) \cite{pmlr-v70-amos17b and GroupMax networks \cite{MR4791896 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Input Convex Neural Networks are neural networks where activation functions are convex, and non-decreasing functions.
elements.sent-2411-013: This design ensures the convexity of the output of the neural network with respect to the inputs as long as weights of the neural network are non-negative.
elements.sent-2411-014: Indeed, it is straightforward that the composition of a non-decreasing convex function with a convex function (actually affine function, representing layers' outputs) is still convex.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this method, the positiveness constraint on weights of the network can be met using penalization or using \q{passthrough layers as suggested in \cite{pmlr-v70-amos17b .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Besides, the representational power of ICNNs is discussed in \cite{pmlr-v70-amos17b , where, to strengthen the representational capacity of ICNNs, it also proposed the use of Partially Input Convex Neural Networks (PICNN) which outputs are convex functions only with respect to some of their inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-017: GroupMax networks builds upon a well-known characterization of convex functions: It can be shown that a function $f : \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R}$ is convex if and only if it is the supremum of the affine functions it dominates.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Thus, the authors designed a neural network architecture that progressively applies the maximum operation to groups of outputs across hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-019: To ensure that the composition of maximum functions preserves convexity, they enforced non-negativity of weights using the function $\mathbb{R \ni x \mapsto \max(x, 0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, the authors established a universal approximation theorem, demonstrating that their network can approximate any convex function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we propose a new Convex Network (CN), allowing to get rid of the positiveness constraint of weights aforementioned (be that for ICNN or GroupMax network), while still producing a convex function.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our architecture has the advantage to be very simple, yet effective.
elements.sent-2411-023: Besides, to strengthen the training phase of our network, we build upon the Deep Linear Network literature \cite{JMLR:v25:23-0493, arora2018a, BahBubacarrLinearNetRiemman .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Linear Neural Networks are networks whose units have no activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus, by construction, and as suggested by their names, they are designed to produce affine functions or, more equivalently, hyperplanes.
elements.sent-2411-026: In their simplest form, they are just one-layer neural networks without activation function.
elements.sent-2411-027: Their deep version obtained by stacking hidden layers without activation functions proved to have good optimization properties.
elements.sent-2411-028: In \cite{JMLR:v25:23-0493 (see also \cite{NIPS2016_f2fc9902 ), the authors studied the loss landscapes in Deep Linear Neural Networks and highlighted challenges like escaping from saddle points.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: They have showed that every critical point of these landscapes that is not a global minimum is a saddle point, meaning stochastic gradient-based methods can theoretically escape them (see \cite{Jin2017HowTE, ge2018learning ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Other works, such as \cite{arora2018a , demonstrate that stochastic gradient descents in deep linear networks converge to a global minimum at a linear rate.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Similarly, \cite{pmlr-v97-du19a shows that with high probability, gradient descent with Xavier initialization \cite{pmlr-v9-glorot10a reaches a global minimum in sufficiently large deep linear networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our network builds on the characterization of convex functions as the supremum of the affine functions they dominate.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this characterization, affine functions may be thought as layers' outputs, and the maximum function can be thought as the activation function of the output layer.
elements.sent-2411-034: Besides, the intuition behind the strength of our network is not only based on the characterization of convex functions aforementioned, but also on the fact that, from the Deep Linear Neural Network literature, we know that stacking layers without activation functions has good optimization properties while producing affine functions (or hyperplanes).
elements.sent-2411-035: Thus, in our Convex Network, the affine part can be replaced by a stack of hidden layers without activation functions, the maximum function serving as the activation function of the output layer.
elements.sent-2411-036: This yields what we will call Scrambling and is closely related to attention mechanism \cite{NIPS2017_3f5ee243 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Indeed, this idea can be compared to the first linear transformation in the attention mechanism, where input vectors are transformed into queries, keys, and values through linear layers.
elements.sent-2411-038: Convex functions may have useful properties, such as having a single global minimum (when coercive), which makes our convex network particularly suitable for applications where convexity is important, such as optimization problems, machine learning, and pricing models.
elements.sent-2411-039: The latter field is the main focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-040: We will demonstrate that our Convex Network is an efficient alternative when it comes to the pricing of options or other financial instruments with convex payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-041: Options pricing is a fundamental problem in Financial Mathematics, where the goal is to determine the fair value of financial derivatives based on their underlying assets.
elements.sent-2411-042: Many of these financial derivatives involve instruments with convex payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-043: Among others, one can cite options like basket \cite{DINGEC2013421 , Best-of-Bermudan \cite{Longstaff2001ValuingAO , and Swing (or Take-or-Pay) options \cite{Thompson1995ValuationOP .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The pricing of last two options relies on respectively Optimal Stopping Theory and Stochastic Optimal Control theory, whereas the pricing of the first option relies on classic Monte Carlo approach.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this paper, we propose the use of our Convex Network for the pricing of such options, a novel approach that leverages the inherent convexity of our Convex Network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-046: Unlike traditional neural networks, which may struggle to maintain convexity properties (sometimes for non-arbitrability purposes) in pricing models, our Convex Network guarantees that this essential condition is automatically met by design.
elements.sent-2411-047: Besides, as mentioned earlier, in this paper, we will tackle three types of options (Basket, Best-of-Bermudan, and Swing) which have convex payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-048: Especially, we consider these options since one can show that, under mild assumptions, their price is a convex function of the underlying asset price.
elements.sent-2411-049: While this is straightforward for basket options, it is more difficult for path-dependent options like Bermudan, and Swing options.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this tougher case, we will rely on convex ordering theory \cite{Pagès2016_cvx_ord_path_dep, pagès2024convexorderingstochasticcontrol .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Then, our aim is to use our Convex Network to approximate prices of such options as a function of the underlying asset value.
elements.sent-2411-052: For Bermudan, and Swing options, this will be based on the Backward Dynamic Programming Principle, where the aim will be to approximate the continuation value which is a conditional expectation.
elements.sent-2411-053: It is worth noting that approximating conditional expectations to solve such kind of problems has already been considered with classic feedforward neural networks (see \cite{MR4308650 ).}
elements.sent-2411-054: The contributions of this paper are threefold.
elements.sent-2411-055: First, we establish theoretical convergence bounds, based on optimal quantization results (see \cite{bookGillesQuantifMarginale, Pagès2018Quantif ), for the approximation of convex functions using our Convex Network, providing a solid foundation for their application in option pricing.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Second, we implement our Convex Network for each of the aforementioned option types and demonstrate its effectiveness through several numerical experiments.
elements.sent-2411-057: Third, we compare the performances of our Convex Network against other state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its advantages in terms of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-059: Section \ref{section_1 presents theoretical foundations of our Convex Network as well as its approximation capacity, providing some error bounds.}
elements.sent-2411-060: We also seize the opportunity to discuss different possible architectures for our Convex Network.
elements.sent-2411-061: In Section \ref{section_2 , we prove the convexity of the prices of the three types of options aforementioned with respect to their underlying asset price.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Section \ref{section_3 presents numerical results, attesting the effectiveness of our Convex Network.. Notations.}

elements.sent-2411-093: This over-parameterization introduces additional degrees of freedom that can have significant implications during training.
elements.sent-2411-094: Specifically, in gradient-based optimization algorithms like gradient descent, the auxiliary parameters are updated iteratively.
elements.sent-2411-095: These updates implicitly influence the effective parameters of the network, allowing for potentially smoother or more efficient convergence dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-096: As a result, while the expressiveness of the model remains unchanged, the optimization landscape may be altered in a way that benefits training.

elements.sent-2411-098: In this case, the linear layers play a critical role in re-parameterizing the input data into different subspaces, each optimized for a specific role in the attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-099: Although the linear transformations in attention are mathematically simple, their impact on the optimization process is substantial.

elements.sent-2411-153: Now, our focus is on the practical side, dealing with option pricing, especially we will consider options with convex payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-154: In financial markets, an option is a financial derivative product giving the right (not the obligation) to its holder to buy (referred to as Call) or sell (referred to as put) a financial asset called the underlying asset.
elements.sent-2411-155: We consider options on multi-assets and assume their dynamics is driven by the Black \& Scholes model, where underlying assets are $d$ risky assets modelled by the following diffusion (in the risk-neutral world) for $i \in \{1,\ldots,d\}$ and $t \ge 0$: \begin{equation}\label{bs_model} \frac{dS_t^i}{S_t^i} = (r-\delta_i) dt + \sigma_i dW_t^i,\quad S_0^i = s_0^i \in \mathbb{R}_{+}^{*},\end{equation} where $r > 0$ is the risk less rate, $\sigma_i > 0$ and $\delta_i > 0$ are volatilities and dividend rates of each asset, and $\big(W_t^1,\ldots,W_t^d\big)$ is a $d$-dimensional Brownian motion with instantaneous correlation $\rho_{i, j}$ for $i,j \in \{1,\ldots, d\}$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Note that, in this model, the risky assets prices are given by: \begin{equation}\label{asset_price_bs_model} S_t^i = s_0^i e^{\big(r - \delta_i - \frac{\sigma_i^2}{2}\big)t + \sigma_i W_t^i}, \quad i \in \{1,\ldots, d\}.\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \SetKwFunction{isOddNumber}{isOddNumber} \SetKwInOut{KwIn}{Input} \SetKwInOut{KwOut}{Output} \SetKwRepeat{Do}{do}{while} \KwIn{ \begin{itemize}[noitemsep] \item Target initial assets prices: $(x_0^1,\ldots,x_0^d)$. \item Model parameters: $r, \sigma_i, T, \rho_{i, j}$. \item Other settings: $K, \alpha_i, M, L, n$. \item Batch size: $B$, number of iterations: $I$. \end{itemize} } \KwOut{$v(x_0^1,\ldots,x_0^d)$.} \tcc{Initialize weights $(\bm{\mathcal{W}}_k)_{0 \le k \le N - 1}$ for $N$ Convex Networks.} \For{$i\gets1$ \KwTo $I$}{ Simulate $B$ i.i.d. paths $\big(S_{t_k}^{1, (b)},\ldots, S_{t_k}^{d, (b)}\big)_{b \in \{1,\ldots, B\}, k \in \{0, \ldots, N\}}$ of $\big(S_{t_k}^{1},\ldots, S_{t_k}^{d}\big)_{\{0, \ldots, N\}}$ starting from $(x_0^1,\ldots,x_0^d)$. Initialize the value function and the optimal stopping time for each path $b \in \{1,\ldots, B\}$: \begin{equation}v_N^{(b)} = g_N\big(S_T^{1, (b)},\ldots, S_T^{d, (b)}\big), \quad \tau_N^{(b)} = N.\end{equation} \For{$k\gets{N -1}$ \KwTo $0$}{ Compute the payoff function and estimate the continuation value via the Convex Network for each path $b \in \{1,\ldots, B\}$: \begin{equation}G_k^{(b)} := g_k\big(S_{t_k}^{1, (b)},\ldots, S_{t_k}^{d, (b)}\big), \quad C_k^{(b)}(W_k) = f_{n,L, k}\big(S_{t_k}^{1, (b)},\ldots, S_{t_k}^{d, (b)}; W_k\big).\end{equation} Update weights $\bm{\mathcal{W}}_k$ via Stochastic Gradient Descent to solve: \begin{equation}\underset{W}{\inf} \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b = 1}^B \big(C_k^{(b)}(W) - v_{k+1}^{(b)}\big)^2.\end{equation} Define the decision function for each path $b \in \{1,\ldots, B\}$: \begin{equation}D_k^{(b)} = \left\{ {ll} 1 \quad if \quad G_k^{(b)} \ge C_k^{(b)}, 0 \quad if \quad G_k^{(b)} < C_k^{(b)}.  \right.\end{equation} Update the optimal stopping time and the value function each path $b \in \{1,\ldots, B\}$: \begin{equation}\tau_k^{(b)} = k \cdot D_k^{(b)} + \tau_{k + 1}^{(b)} \cdot \big(1 - D_k^{(b)}\big), \quad v_k^{(b)} = G_k^{(b)} \cdot D_k^{(b)} + v_{k+1}^{(b)}\cdot \big(1 - D_k^{(b)}\big).\end{equation} } Compute estimate of the initial value: \begin{equation}\hat{v}_0 = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b = 1}^B v_0^{(b)}.\end{equation} } \KwRet{$\hat{v}_0$.} \caption{Convex Network training: Best-of Bermuda Call option pricing.} \label{algo_icnn_bermuda_option}}
elements.sent-2411-222: We now consider a (discrete time) Stochastic Optimal Control (SOC) problem over a (discrete) time grid $0=t_0 < t_1 < \ldots < t_N = T$.
elements.sent-2411-223: Similar to the optimal stopping time problem studied in the previous section, this problem can be think as a stochastic game where a gambler pays an initial amount $v_0$ for the right to take actions $q_k$, at times $t_k$, and receives a reward (which can also be a loss) $g_k$.
elements.sent-2411-224: The latter reward depends on both the gambler's action and a random vector $X_{t_k}$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Here, we consider a constrained version of the game meaning the gambler's actions are restricted to $\big(q_0,\ldots, q_N\big) \in \mathcal{S}$, with $\mathcal{S}$ being the constraint space.
elements.sent-2411-226: Depending on how the set $\mathcal{S}$ is defined, it may prevent for trivial optimal strategies where the gambler may take the action whenever it suits her and do nothing otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-227: A typical and more interesting (in a modelling standpoint) case would be a set $\mathcal{S}$ designed in a way that it might compel the gambler to take an action even when it does not suit her.
elements.sent-2411-228: This framework as described embeds several well-studied real world problems \cite{BarreraEsteve2006NumericalMF, Fleming1975, Bertsekas_stoch_control .}
elements.sent-2411-229: Among these problems, we have decided to study the pricing of Take-or-Pay or Swing options.
elements.sent-2411-230: A Take-or-Pay (or Swing) option is a commodity derivative product which enable its holder to purchase amounts of energy (generally gas delivery contract) $q_k$, at predetermined exercise dates, $t_k, k \in \{0,\ldots,N-1\}$ (thus $N$ exercise dates), until the contract maturity at time $t_N = T$.
elements.sent-2411-231: The purchase price, or strike price, is supposed to be fixed for all dates and is denoted as $K$.
elements.sent-2411-232: The holder of the swing contract ought to have a buying/consumption strategy satisfying the following constraints: \begin{equation}\label{swing_vol_constraints} \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} q \le q_{k} \le \overline{q}, \quad k \in \{0,\ldots, N-1\}, \displaystyle Q_{N} := \sum_{k = 0}^{N-1} q_{k} \in \big[Q, \overline{Q}\big], \quad Q_0 = 0, \quad 0 \le Q \le \overline{Q} < +\infty. \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}
